expanded_description: Natural selection operates in different patterns depending on environmental pressures and population characteristics. These different types of selection—directional, stabilizing, and disruptive—affect trait distributions in populations in distinct ways, shaping evolutionary outcomes and maintaining or reducing genetic variation within populations.

core_concept.principle: Different types of natural selection favor different phenotypes within a population, producing distinct patterns of evolutionary change and affecting genetic variation in characteristic ways.
core_concept.explanation: Natural selection is not a single uniform process—it operates in different modes depending on which phenotypes have highest fitness. Directional selection favors one extreme phenotype, stabilizing selection favors intermediate phenotypes, and disruptive selection favors both extremes. Each type produces predictable changes in trait distributions and has different consequences for population variation and adaptation.

image.alt: Diagram comparing directional, stabilizing, and disruptive selection showing trait distribution changes
image.caption: Figure 10.6: Three main types of natural selection produce distinct patterns of trait distribution change in populations.

sections.section_title: Types of Natural Selection and Their Effects
sections.introduction: Natural selection can operate in different modes depending on environmental conditions and which phenotypes confer highest fitness. Understanding these different selection types helps predict how populations will evolve, how variation will be maintained or reduced, and what adaptations are likely to emerge. Each selection type leaves a characteristic signature on population trait distributions.

key_concept.definition: Types of natural selection refer to different patterns of differential survival and reproduction that favor different phenotypes within a population, producing distinct evolutionary outcomes.
key_concept.context: Selection type depends on environmental pressures, resource availability, predation patterns, and population characteristics; can change over time as environment changes; affects genetic variation differently.

image.alt: Conceptual diagram showing how different selection types affect population trait distributions
image.caption: Different selection types favor different phenotypes, changing population trait distributions in predictable ways.

classifications.value: Selection that favors individuals at one extreme of the phenotypic range, shifting population mean in one direction
characteristics: Common during environmental change or colonization
classifications.behavior: Individuals with traits at one end of the distribution have higher fitness; allele frequencies shift consistently in one direction; population becomes better adapted to new conditions

examples.explanation: During Industrial Revolution, dark-colored moths had higher survival on soot-covered trees; population shifted from predominantly light to predominantly dark; reversed when pollution decreased

image.alt: Peppered moth color shift during industrialization
image.caption: Peppered moth evolution demonstrates directional selection in response to environmental change.

examples.explanation: Bacteria with resistance genes survive antibiotic treatment and reproduce; population shifts from sensitive to resistant; major medical concern demonstrating rapid directional selection

image.alt: Bacterial population evolving antibiotic resistance
image.caption: Antibiotic resistance evolves through directional selection favoring resistant bacteria.

examples.explanation: Longer-necked giraffes could reach higher foliage during food scarcity; had higher survival and reproduction; population evolved longer average neck length over generations

image.alt: Giraffe neck length evolution through directional selection
image.caption: Giraffe neck evolution demonstrates directional selection for increased reach.

classifications.value: Selection that favors intermediate phenotypes and selects against both extremes, maintaining population mean
classifications.behavior: Individuals with intermediate traits have highest fitness; extreme phenotypes have lower survival; variation decreases while mean remains stable; maintains adaptation to current environment

examples.explanation: Babies of intermediate weight (3-4 kg) have highest survival; very small babies have health complications; very large babies have birth difficulties; stabilizing selection maintains optimal birth weight

image.alt: Human birth weight survival curve showing stabilizing selection
image.caption: Human birth weight demonstrates stabilizing selection favoring intermediate values.

examples.explanation: Birds laying intermediate number of eggs have most surviving offspring; too few eggs = low reproductive output; too many eggs = insufficient parental care; optimal clutch size maintained

image.alt: Bird clutch size showing stabilizing selection pattern
image.caption: Bird clutch size demonstrates stabilizing selection for optimal reproductive output.

examples.explanation: Plants of intermediate height have best balance of light capture and wind resistance; very short plants shaded out; very tall plants damaged by wind; intermediate height maintained

image.alt: Plant height distribution under stabilizing selection
image.caption: Plant height demonstrates stabilizing selection in stable environments.

classifications.value: Selection that favors individuals at both extremes of the phenotypic range while selecting against intermediate phenotypes
classifications.behavior: Individuals with extreme traits have higher fitness than intermediates; population may split into two distinct groups; can produce bimodal trait distribution; may lead to reproductive isolation and speciation

examples.explanation: Finches with large beaks crack hard seeds; finches with small beaks eat soft seeds; intermediate beaks inefficient for both; population shows bimodal beak size distribution

image.alt: Seedcracker finch beak sizes showing bimodal distribution
image.caption: African seedcracker finches demonstrate disruptive selection on beak size.

examples.explanation: Large male salmon fight for mates; small male salmon sneak fertilizations; intermediate-sized males unsuccessful at both strategies; both extremes favored

image.alt: Salmon size distribution showing disruptive selection
image.caption: Salmon mating strategies demonstrate disruptive selection on body size.

examples.process: Light and dark moths on mixed substrate
examples.explanation: In habitats with both light and dark surfaces, both light and dark moths are camouflaged; intermediate-colored moths visible on both surfaces; both extremes favored

image.alt: Moth coloration showing disruptive selection pattern
image.caption: Moth coloration can demonstrate disruptive selection in heterogeneous environments.

principles.statement: The type of selection acting on a population depends on environmental conditions and which phenotypes confer highest fitness in that context.
principles.application: Explains why selection type can change when environment changes; predicts how populations will respond to environmental change; important for conservation planning.
principles.example: Peppered moths experienced directional selection toward dark color during industrialization, then directional selection back toward light color when pollution decreased.
principles.analogy: Like fashion trends—what's favored changes with the times; selection favors different traits as environment changes.

image.alt: Diagram showing how environmental change alters selection type
image.caption: Environmental conditions determine which selection type operates on a population.

principles.principle: Variation Effects Differ by Selection Type
principles.statement: Different selection types have different effects on genetic variation: directional and stabilizing reduce variation, while disruptive increases or maintains variation.
principles.application: Predicts how selection affects population's evolutionary potential; explains why some populations maintain high variation while others don't; important for conservation genetics.
principles.example: Stabilizing selection on human birth weight reduces variation; disruptive selection on finch beak size maintains high variation in the population.
principles.analogy: Like filtering sand—different筛子 (sieves) remove different grain sizes; selection types filter variation differently.

image.alt: How different selection types affect population variation
image.caption: Different selection types have distinct effects on genetic variation.

principles.principle: Selection Type Can Change Over Time
principles.statement: The same trait may experience different types of selection at different times as environmental conditions change.
principles.application: Explains complex evolutionary patterns; predicts population responses to environmental fluctuations; important for understanding evolutionary dynamics.
principles.example: Galápagos finch beak size experiences directional selection during drought (favoring larger beaks), stabilizing selection during normal years (maintaining optimal size).
principles.analogy: Like a thermostat adjusting to seasons—selection adjusts to environmental conditions, changing which traits are favored.

image.alt: Selection type changing over time with environmental conditions
image.caption: Selection type can shift as environmental conditions change over time.

components.definition: Types of natural selection are classified by which phenotypes have highest fitness and how trait distributions change in response to selective pressures.
components.interpretation: Selection differential measures strength and direction of selection; positive values indicate selection for larger trait values; magnitude indicates selection strength; varies by selection type.

image.alt: Components defining different selection types
image.caption: Selection types are defined by fitness-phenotype relationships and trait distribution changes.

categories.description: Different selection types produce characteristic relationships between phenotype and fitness
categories.detailed_mechanism: Fitness functions describe how survival and reproduction vary with phenotype; directional = linear relationship; stabilizing = peak at intermediate; disruptive = valley at intermediate

examples.explanation: Fitness increases or decreases linearly with trait value; one extreme has highest fitness; produces consistent directional change in population mean

image.alt: Linear fitness function for directional selection
image.caption: Directional selection produces linear fitness-phenotype relationship.

examples.explanation: Fitness peaks at intermediate trait values; decreases toward both extremes; maintains population at optimal phenotype

image.alt: Peak fitness function for stabilizing selection
image.caption: Stabilizing selection produces peak fitness at intermediate phenotypes.

examples.explanation: Fitness lowest at intermediate trait values; highest at both extremes; can split population into distinct groups

image.alt: Valley fitness function for disruptive selection
image.caption: Disruptive selection produces lowest fitness at intermediate phenotypes.

image.alt: Comparison of fitness functions for different selection types
image.caption: Different selection types produce distinct fitness-phenotype relationships.

categories.description: Selection types can be visualized through trait distribution graphs showing before and after selection
categories.detailed_mechanism: Bell curves show trait distribution; selection changes curve shape and position; directional shifts curve, stabilizing narrows curve, disruptive splits curve

examples.explanation: Bell curve shifts left or right over generations; mean changes; variation may decrease slightly; shows adaptation to new conditions

image.alt: Trait distribution graph showing directional selection
image.caption: Directional selection shifts trait distribution in one direction.

examples.explanation: Bell curve becomes narrower and taller; mean stays same; variation decreases; shows maintenance of optimal phenotype

image.alt: Trait distribution graph showing stabilizing selection
image.caption: Stabilizing selection narrows trait distribution around the mean.

examples.explanation: Single bell curve splits into two peaks; mean may stay same but becomes meaningless; variation increases; shows population divergence

image.alt: Trait distribution graph showing disruptive selection
image.caption: Disruptive selection splits trait distribution into two distinct groups.

image.alt: Comparison of graphical representations for all selection types
image.caption: Graphical representations clearly show how different selection types affect trait distributions.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Selection Type from Data
worked_examples.problem: A researcher measures beak depth in a bird population over 5 generations. Generation 1: mean = 8.5mm, variance = 2.1. Generation 5: mean = 10.2mm, variance = 1.8. What type of selection is occurring, and what does this suggest about the environment?

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze the changes: mean increased from 8.5mm to 10.2mm (shift in one direction); variance decreased from 2.1 to 1.8 (slight reduction in variation).
Match pattern to selection types: directional selection shifts mean in one direction; stabilizing keeps mean same; disruptive increases variance.
Conclude: Pattern matches directional selection (mean shifted, variance slightly reduced). Environment likely changed to favor larger beaks (e.g., larger seeds available).

worked_examples.final_answer: Directional selection is occurring, favoring larger beak depth. The environment likely changed to favor birds with larger beaks, possibly due to availability of larger or harder seeds that require stronger beaks to crack.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Selection type can be identified from changes in population mean and variance over generations; directional selection shifts mean, stabilizing reduces variance, disruptive increases variance.

image.alt: Graph showing beak depth change over generations
image.caption: Trait distribution changes reveal the type of selection acting on a population.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Selection Response
worked_examples.problem: In a population of plants, height ranges from 10-50 cm. If a new herbivore arrives that preferentially eats plants 25-35 cm tall (can reach that height), what type of selection will occur and how will the population change?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify which phenotypes have highest fitness: short plants (<25 cm) escape herbivore; tall plants (>35 cm) escape herbivore; intermediate plants (25-35 cm) heavily eaten.
Match fitness pattern to selection type: both extremes favored, intermediates selected against = disruptive selection.
Predict population change: population will develop bimodal height distribution; variation will increase; may eventually split into two distinct groups if reproductive isolation develops.

worked_examples.final_answer: Disruptive selection will occur, favoring both short and tall plants while selecting against intermediate heights. The population will develop a bimodal height distribution with increased variation, potentially leading to speciation if the two groups become reproductively isolated.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Disruptive selection occurs when both extreme phenotypes have higher fitness than intermediates; produces bimodal distributions and can lead to speciation.

image.alt: Plant height distribution before and after disruptive selection
image.caption: Disruptive selection produces bimodal trait distributions.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Selection Strength
worked_examples.problem: In a population, the mean trait value before selection is 50 units. After selection, the mean of reproducing individuals is 55 units. If heritability (h²) is 0.4, what is the expected response to selection (R) in the next generation?

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate selection differential (S): S = mean of selected parents - mean of population before selection.
Apply breeder's equation: R = h² × S, where R = response to selection, h² = heritability, S = selection differential.
Interpret: Next generation mean will increase by 2 units; new mean = 50 + 2 = 52 units; shows directional selection response.

worked_examples.final_answer: The expected response to selection is R = 2 units. The next generation's mean trait value will be 52 units (increased from 50). This demonstrates directional selection with quantifiable evolutionary response.
worked_examples.concept_applied: The breeder's equation (R = h²S) quantifies evolutionary response to selection; response depends on both selection strength (S) and heritability (h²).

image.alt: Calculation of selection response using breeder's equation
image.caption: The breeder's equation predicts evolutionary response to selection.

sections.role: Patterns of Differential Survival and Reproduction

components.composition: Selection favoring one extreme phenotype; linear fitness-phenotype relationship; shifts population mean
components.function: Adapts populations to new environmental conditions; produces consistent evolutionary change in one direction
components.key_feature: Population mean changes over time; common during environmental change or colonization of new habitats

image.alt: Directional selection structure and effects
image.caption: Directional selection shifts population trait distributions in one direction.

components.composition: Selection favoring intermediate phenotypes; peak fitness function; reduces population variation
components.function: Maintains adaptation to stable environments; prevents deviation from optimal phenotype; preserves existing adaptations
components.key_feature: Population mean stays stable; variation decreases; most common selection type in stable environments

image.alt: Stabilizing selection structure and effects
image.caption: Stabilizing selection maintains optimal phenotypes and reduces variation.

components.composition: Selection favoring both extreme phenotypes; valley fitness function; increases or maintains variation
components.function: Can produce population divergence; may lead to speciation; maintains multiple adaptive strategies in heterogeneous environments
components.key_feature: Can produce bimodal distributions; increases variation; rarest selection type but important for speciation

image.alt: Disruptive selection structure and effects
image.caption: Disruptive selection favors extreme phenotypes and can split populations.

function.operation: Different selection types function by favoring different phenotypes, producing distinct patterns of trait distribution change and affecting genetic variation in characteristic ways.
function.energy: Selection itself requires no energy input; however, the biological processes underlying differential survival and reproduction depend on organismal metabolism and resource acquisition
function.mechanism: Environmental pressures create fitness differences among phenotypes; differential reproduction changes allele frequencies; trait distributions shift according to selection type

image.alt: Overview of how different selection types function
image.caption: Different selection types function through distinct fitness-phenotype relationships.

cycle.operation: Different phenotypes have different survival and reproductive success based on environmental conditions
cycle.purpose: Create selective pressure that favors certain phenotypes over others

image.alt: Fitness variation among phenotypes
image.caption: Fitness variation among phenotypes drives selection.

cycle.operation: Individuals with higher fitness contribute more offspring to next generation; allele frequencies change
cycle.purpose: Translate fitness differences into genetic change; shift population trait distribution

image.alt: Differential reproduction changing allele frequencies
image.caption: Differential reproduction translates fitness into evolutionary change.

cycle.operation: Population trait distribution changes according to selection type: shifts (directional), narrows (stabilizing), or splits (disruptive)
cycle.purpose: Adapt population to environment; maintain optimal phenotypes; or produce divergence

image.alt: Trait distribution changes under different selection types
image.caption: Trait distributions change in predictable ways under different selection types.

process_flow.description: Environmental conditions create differential survival and reproduction among individuals with different phenotypes.
process_flow.mechanism: Predation, competition, climate, resources create selective pressures; some phenotypes better suited to current conditions than others.
process_flow.result: Fitness variation established among phenotypes; selection type determined by which phenotypes have highest fitness.

image.alt: Environmental pressures creating fitness differences
image.caption: Environmental pressures determine which selection type operates.

process_flow.process: Selection Type Determined by Fitness Pattern
process_flow.description: Pattern of fitness across phenotypic range determines whether selection is directional, stabilizing, or disruptive.
process_flow.mechanism: Linear fitness function = directional; peak fitness function = stabilizing; valley fitness function = disruptive.
process_flow.result: Selection type identified; predicts how trait distribution will change; determines effect on variation.

image.alt: Fitness patterns determining selection type
image.caption: Fitness-phenotype relationships determine selection type.

process_flow.description: Population trait distribution changes according to selection type over generations.
process_flow.mechanism: Directional: mean shifts; Stabilizing: variance decreases; Disruptive: distribution splits or becomes bimodal.
process_flow.result: Population adapts to environment; variation affected differently by each selection type; evolutionary change occurs.

image.alt: Trait distribution changes under different selection types
image.caption: Different selection types produce distinct trait distribution changes.

process_flow.description: Each selection type has characteristic effect on population genetic variation.
process_flow.mechanism: Directional: reduces variation at one end; Stabilizing: reduces overall variation; Disruptive: maintains or increases variation.
process_flow.result: Population's evolutionary potential affected; future adaptive capacity determined; conservation implications.

image.alt: Effects of different selection types on variation
image.caption: Selection types have distinct effects on population genetic variation.

process_flow.description: Continued selection produces long-term evolutionary changes specific to selection type.
process_flow.mechanism: Directional: adaptation to new conditions; Stabilizing: maintenance of optimal phenotype; Disruptive: potential speciation through population divergence.
process_flow.result: Populations adapted to environments; biodiversity patterns shaped; evolutionary trajectories determined.

image.alt: Long-term evolutionary outcomes of different selection types
image.caption: Different selection types produce distinct long-term evolutionary outcomes.

process_types.energy_profile: Often energetically costly; direction: can be directional, stabilizing, or disruptive depending on mating system
process_types.direction: Favors traits that increase mating success, sometimes even if they reduce survival
process_types.function: Special form of natural selection acting on reproductive success; can produce extreme traits and sexual dimorphism

examples.explanation: One sex (usually females) chooses mates based on traits; e.g., peacock tail displays, bird songs, bright coloration; can produce elaborate ornaments

image.alt: Peacock tail demonstrating intersexual selection
image.caption: Intersexual selection produces elaborate mating displays and ornaments.

examples.explanation: Members of one sex (usually males) compete for access to mates; e.g., deer antlers, elephant seal size, beetle horns; produces weapons and large size

image.alt: Male deer fighting demonstrating intrasexual selection
image.caption: Intrasexual selection produces weapons and competitive traits.

image.alt: Overview of sexual selection types and examples
image.caption: Sexual selection is a special form of natural selection acting on reproductive success.

process_types.energy_profile: Maintains variation; direction: preserves multiple alleles in population
process_types.direction: Maintains genetic variation rather than fixing single allele; opposite of directional selection
process_types.function: Preserves genetic variation in populations; maintains multiple adaptive strategies; important for disease resistance and environmental variability

examples.explanation: Heterozygotes have higher fitness than either homozygote; e.g., sickle cell allele in malaria regions (AS heterozygotes resistant to malaria); maintains both alleles in population

image.alt: Sickle cell heterozygote advantage in malaria regions
image.caption: Heterozygote advantage maintains genetic variation through balancing selection.

examples.explanation: Fitness depends on allele frequency; rare alleles favored; e.g., prey color morphs, self-incompatibility in plants; maintains multiple alleles

image.alt: Frequency-dependent selection maintaining variation
image.caption: Frequency-dependent selection maintains variation by favoring rare alleles.

image.alt: Overview of balancing selection mechanisms
image.caption: Balancing selection maintains genetic variation through multiple mechanisms.

sections.definition: Types of natural selection are classified by which phenotypes have highest fitness and how trait distributions change in response.
sections.purpose: To understand how different patterns of selection produce distinct evolutionary outcomes, affect genetic variation differently, and shape population adaptation to environments.

activation.definition: Selection type is activated when environmental conditions create specific fitness-phenotype relationships that favor certain phenotypes over others.
activation.effect: Population trait distributions change in predictable ways; variation affected differently; evolutionary trajectory determined by selection type.

image.alt: Activation of different selection types by environmental conditions
image.caption: Environmental conditions activate specific selection types.

key_properties.explanation: Directional and stabilizing selection reduce genetic variation; disruptive and balancing selection maintain or increase variation; affects population's future evolutionary potential.

image.alt: Different selection type effects on variation
image.caption: Selection types have distinct effects on population genetic variation.

key_properties.explanation: Same trait may experience different selection types at different times as environment changes; explains complex evolutionary patterns and population responses to environmental fluctuation.

image.alt: Selection type changing over time
image.caption: Selection type can shift as environmental conditions change.

key_properties.property: Multiple Selection Types Can Act Simultaneously
key_properties.explanation: Different traits in same population may experience different selection types; same trait may experience multiple selection pressures; net effect determines evolutionary outcome.

image.alt: Multiple selection types acting simultaneously
image.caption: Multiple selection types can act on populations simultaneously.

key_properties.explanation: Knowing selection type allows prediction of how population will evolve: directional = adaptation to new conditions; stabilizing = maintenance of status quo; disruptive = potential speciation.

image.alt: Predictive power of selection type identification
image.caption: Selection type identification enables prediction of evolutionary outcomes.

summary: Natural selection operates in different patterns that affect populations in distinct ways. Directional selection favors one extreme phenotype, shifting population mean in one direction (e.g., antibiotic resistance, peppered moths); reduces variation and adapts populations to new conditions. Stabilizing selection favors intermediate phenotypes, maintaining population mean while reducing variation (e.g., human birth weight, bird clutch size); common in stable environments. Disruptive selection favors both extremes while selecting against intermediates, increasing variation and potentially producing bimodal distributions (e.g., African seedcracker finches, salmon mating strategies); can lead to speciation. Sexual selection acts on reproductive success through mate choice (intersexual) or competition (intrasexual), sometimes producing extreme traits. Balancing selection maintains genetic variation through heterozygote advantage or frequency-dependent selection. Selection type depends on environmental conditions and can change over time; different types have different effects on variation and evolutionary potential. Understanding selection types enables prediction of evolutionary outcomes, interpretation of trait distribution patterns, and conservation planning. Selection types connect natural selection mechanisms (Section 10.3) to adaptation patterns (Section 10.4), genetic variation (Section 10.5), and speciation processes (Section 10.7), showing how different selection patterns produce different evolutionary outcomes.
